quick_facts.label: National occupational standard
quick_facts.value: Commis Chef is mapped to Tourism & Hospitality Skill Council qualification pack THC/Q0406, version 2.0, at NSQF Level 4.

quick_facts.label: Role focus
quick_facts.value: The qualification pack describes the role as setting up the kitchen, assisting with food preparation and cooking, preparing base items such as sauces, salads and cold starters, monitoring stock, and closing down after a shift.

quick_facts.label: Occupational setting
quick_facts.value: The standard places the role in the Hotels/Restaurant subsector and the Food Production and Kitchen occupation.

quick_facts.label: Published entry benchmark
quick_facts.value: The accessible THC/Q0406 version 2.0 lists 12th class or a two-year ITI after class 10; alternatively, a one-year ITI after class 10 plus one year of food-production experience, or an NSQF Level 3 Assistant Chef certificate. It lists a minimum job-entry age of 18. Its own review/deactivation fields are dated January 29, 2024, so applicants should confirm current provider and employer requirements.

quick_facts.label: Relevant formal study routes
quick_facts.value: NCHMCT offers a 1.5-year Diploma in Food Production and a 1.5-year Diploma in Bakery & Confectionery, each described as open to 10+2 pass students with English and including 24 weeks of industrial training. Its B.Sc. in Hospitality & Hotel Administration is a three-year programme with food-production laboratory training.

sections.heading: What a Commis Chef does
paragraphs: A Commis Chef is an operational, junior kitchen role rather than a kitchen-management post. Under the THC/Q0406 occupational standard, the role begins with mise en place: organising the assigned station, arranging tools and equipment, checking cleanliness and functionality, monitoring appliance temperatures, and preparing the station for the next shift.

During production, the Commis Chef selects and measures ingredients; washes, peels and chops produce; seasons meat; performs basic preparation such as grinding spices, making dough and wrapping items; prepares sauces, salads and cold starters; and plates food to the Chef de Partie’s instructions. The role also supports receiving and checking deliveries, labelling and rotating stock, correct storage, sanitation of workstations and equipment, safe disposal of waste, and end-of-shift close-down. In kitchens using a tandoor, the same standard includes preparing, using, cleaning and maintaining that equipment.
bullets: Prepare and restock a clean, correctly equipped workstation.
Carry out recipe-led prep, cooking and plating under the direction of senior kitchen staff.
Check deliveries, label products, rotate stock and report inventory needs.
Follow food-safety, hygiene, temperature-control, cleaning and waste-management procedures.
Communicate clearly with colleagues, superiors and, where required, guests.

sections.heading: Entry routes from Indian education and skills pathways
paragraphs: A direct skills route is the THC/Q0406 Commis Chef qualification pathway. The published version 2.0 sets out school/ITI or Assistant Chef certificate alternatives and a minimum job-entry age of 18. Because that document itself shows a January 29, 2024 next-review/deactivation date, it should be used as a role benchmark rather than treated as a guarantee of a current admission or recruitment rule; verify eligibility, assessment and placement conditions with the training provider or employer.

The node’s Diploma in Food Production parent is a particularly aligned route. NCHMCT describes it as a 1.5-year programme for 10+2 students with English, comprising 36 weeks of coursework followed by 24 weeks of industrial training; its published teaching scheme includes cookery, larder, hygiene and sanitation, nutrition, commodities, food costing, and practical cookery. The Diploma in Bakery & Confectionery has the same published 10+2-with-English and 24-week industrial-training structure for candidates aiming at pastry and bakery-oriented kitchens.

For a broader hospitality foundation, NCHMCT’s B.Sc. in Hospitality & Hotel Administration is a three-year programme with laboratory training in food production alongside food and beverage service, front office and housekeeping. It is oriented toward wider operational and supervisory hospitality capability, so it can be a longer route into kitchen work rather than a requirement for every Commis Chef vacancy.
bullets: Skills route: pursue a recognised Commis Chef/food-production training programme and seek supervised kitchen experience.
Food-production route: complete an NCHMCT-affiliated Diploma in Food Production, including industrial training where offered.
Bakery route: complete a Diploma in Bakery & Confectionery for bakery, pastry and confectionery exposure.
Broader route: complete B.Sc. Hospitality & Hotel Administration when seeking multi-department hospitality preparation in addition to food production.

sections.heading: Skills to build
paragraphs: Core technical capability is practical and station-based: ingredient selection and measurement, knife and preparation work, marination, dough preparation, basic sauces and cold-food preparation, cooking methods, plating, operation of kitchen appliances, stock rotation and safe storage. The standard also identifies tandoor work as a competency area, including bread, roasted meat or vegetables, marinades, heat awareness and cleaning, where that equipment is used.

Food safety is central to employability. THC/Q0406 expects knowledge of food hygiene, quality and safety, HACCP-related occupational health and safety, sanitation, storage, cooking temperatures and safe handling of sharp tools. FSSAI’s FoSTaC materials likewise identify general hygienic and sanitary practices and personal hygiene for food handlers as key training areas. Communication, ability to read work instructions, teamwork, attention to detail, patience, creativity and stamina for long working hours are also expressly identified in the role materials.
bullets: Mise en place, recipe measurement, preparation and basic cooking techniques.
Safe use and cleaning of knives, equipment, appliances and, where relevant, tandoors.
Food hygiene, sanitation, controlled storage, stock rotation and waste handling.
Quality checks, portion consistency and plating to senior-chef instructions.
Clear communication, discipline, time management and resilience during service.

sections.heading: Where the work is done
paragraphs: The national qualification pack locates Commis Chef work in hotels and restaurants, within food production and kitchen operations. In practice, the kitchen may be organised into stations or sections, with a junior chef assisting the chef responsible for that station; NCHMCT’s food-production material illustrates this brigade model through examples such as supporting fish or sauce preparation under the relevant senior cook.

Related food-production training is used across hotels, restaurants, fast-food outlets, resorts, railways, airlines and cruise lines, according to NCHMCT. Actual duties, shift patterns, cuisine specialization, equipment and guest interaction vary with the size and type of establishment. The role is therefore best understood as hands-on kitchen production work carried out under senior culinary supervision.
bullets: Hotels and standalone restaurants.
Fast-food outlets, resorts and institutional or transport-linked catering operations.
Specialist stations such as hot kitchen, cold kitchen, bakery/pastry, larder or tandoor, depending on the employer.
Shift-based, sanitation-sensitive work that requires standing, coordination and timely service.

sections.heading: Progression context
paragraphs: A common kitchen-brigade progression is from junior commis responsibilities toward a defined station role and then supervisory responsibility. NCHMCT explains that Chef de Partie roles are working-cook positions responsible for defined kitchen sections, and that junior chefs support work at their assigned stations.

As an indicative skills-framework reference, the THSC Demi Chef de Partie qualification pack at NSQF Level 5 lists either a three-year diploma plus three years’ Commis Chef experience, or an NSQF Level 4 Commis Chef certificate plus two years’ Commis Chef experience. That particular document records a December 30, 2024 review/deactivation date; it is useful for understanding the intended progression but is not a current universal promotion rule. Employers may use different titles, experience thresholds and internal assessments.
bullets: Build reliable performance in prep, hygiene, service and stock handling.
Develop competence in a particular kitchen station or cuisine.
Progress toward station-level responsibility such as Demi Chef de Partie or Chef de Partie, subject to employer structure and demonstrated experience.
Confirm current qualification and promotion criteria directly with the training body or employer.
